content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
August 21,
1995
Scott Cauchois
Public Utilities Commission
505 Van Ness Avenue
Division of Ratepayer Advocates
Telecommunications Branch
San Francisco,
CA
94102—3298
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95-233
Dear Mr.
Cauchois:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
May you participate in reviewing a project which may
financially affect your spouse’s employer?
CONCLUSION
You are required to disqualify yourself from participating in
any governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-95-233
Page
2
and material financial effect on your spouse’s employer,
as set
forth in Regulation 18702.2.
FACTS
You are a Program and Project Supervisor in the Division of
Ratepayer Advocates
(DEA),
Telecommunications Branch, within the
California Public Utilities Commission
(CPUC).
Recently, your
wife accepted employment with the Teleport Communications Group
(TCG),
a certified competitive access provider and intraLATA toll
carrier.
Your spouse is a salaried employee of TCG.
TCG is currently a competitor of Pacific Bell
(Pac Bell) for
some services,
and may become a competitor of Pac Bell for local
telephone exchange services if competition is introduced in that
area in the future.
Although the CPtJC has no regulatory authority
over TCG’s rates, the CPUC regulates the rates and terms and
conditions of service for many of Pacific Bell’s services.
TCG is
certificated by the CPUC,
however.
You will be working on a telecommunications assignment, the
1995-96 New Regulatory Framework
(NRF) review.
NRF is an
incentive-based regulatory framework that replaced the traditional
cost—of—service regulation in 1990.
The 1995—96 NRF review
applies to Pac Bell and GTE California,
and involves a review of
such issues as the price cap mechanism, productivity factor, rate
of return,
earnings sharing, telephone service affordability,
and
service quality.
The effect on Pacific Bell and GTE California
will primarily relate to changes in the productivity factor,
changing the earnings sharing formula,
and otherwise altering the
price cap mechanism.
Such changes will have no direct effect on
TCG.
However, the changes to the NRF incentive mechanism may
affect the rates for telephone services offered by Pacific Bell,
in-competition with--TCG,
and thus,
could have an indirect impact
onTCG.
-
--
Your primary assignment is to provide supervisorial oversight
in this proceeding for DRA.
In that context,
you will be engaging
in discussion of project planning, providing policy direction,
and
meeting with top CPUC management.
You will also be responsible.
for review of written testimony and may,
in addition,
be directly
responsible for writing a part of DRA’s testimony.
TCG is not intervening in this case directly.
However,
TCG
is a member of an informal coalition (the “Coalition”)
which is
intervening.
The Coalition was formed on an ad hoc basis in late
1994 to participate in a number of telecommunications proceedings
such as local competition, presubscription,
and open network
architecture.
Although the Coalition did not initiate, nor is the
subject of, the proceeding concerning PacBell and GTE California
it has decided to continue its efforts to represent the
telecommunications and consumer interests of its members for the
NRF proceeding.


Our File No.
A-95-233
Page
3
The Coalition represents the following:
AT&T Communications
of California,
MCI Telecommunications, Sprint Communications and
other major long distance telephone service providers; the
California Association of Long Distance Companies
(representing
approximately 100 small and medium—sized long distance companies);
the California Cable Television Association (representing numerous
cable TV companies operating in California); the California
Committee for Large Telecommunications Consumers
(representing
major banks and others businesses); Toward Utility Rate
Normalization (representing millions of residential and small
business costumers); the California Payphone Association;
ICG
Access Services:
MFS Intelnet; Teleport Communications Group
(TCG);
and Time Warner AxS of California.
Functionally,
individual members of the Coalition do not
always agree with the Coalition’s positions,
and, therefore, at
any given time,
a member may opt out of a formal filing by the
Coalition.
ANALYSIS
You are a public official under the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that a public official may not make a
decision, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on any of various financial
interests of the official, including:
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
receivethby or promised to the public official
withIn
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(c).
In addition,
Section 82030 provides that the income of an
individual also includes any community property interet in the
income of a spouse.
Presumably,
TCG has been a source of income
to your spouse in an amount of $500 or more
(making your community
property interest $250) within the past 12 months.
Therefore,
TCG
is a potentially disqualifying interest of yours as described in
Section 87103(c),
and you are prohibited from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using your official position
to influence a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on TCG which is
distinguishable from the decision’s effect on the public
generally.


Our File No.
A-95--233
Page
4
Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
It appears reasonably foreseeable that TCG will be affected
by the review decisions.
You have stated that it will be affected
by decisions concerning the 1995-96 New Regulatory Framework
review because its primary competitor for services, Pac Bell, is
likely to be financially affected by the decisions.
This may
occur, for example, if the decisions affect Pac Bell’s profit
sharing.
Materiality
To require your disqualification, however, the effect on TCG
must also be material.
The Commission has adopted differing
guidelines to determine whether an effect is material,
depending
on whether a source of income is directly or indirectly involved
in a decision.
Where a source of income is directly involved in a decision
before you,
you may not participate in the decision.
(Regulation
18702.1(a).)
A source of income is directly involved in a
decision if the source of income initiates the proceeding by
filing an application,
claim, appeal,
or similar request,
or is a
named party in,
or the subject of the proceeding.
A source of
income is the subject of the proceeding if it involves the
issuance, renewal,
approval, denial_or revocation oLany license,
permit, or Other entitlement to,
or contract with, the subject
-
person or business entity.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
According to your facts, the proceeding in question was not
initiated by the Coalition nor TCG.
Moreover, the proceeding
involves Pac Bell and GTE California, which are not members.of the
Coalition.
Theref&e, the Coalition members are not named
applicants or the subject of the proceeding.
In your letter,
you have indicated that while TCG will not be
directly involved in the decision, it will be indirectly affected
by decisions concerning the 1995-96 New Regulatory Framework
review.
Decisions which indirectly affect a business entity
identified as a financial interest of a public official are
material when they have the effect of increasing or decreasing the
gross revenue, assets, liabilities, or expenses of the business
entity by the relevant amounts identified in Regulation 18702.2
(copy enclosed).


Our File No.
A—95-233
Page
5
Assuming, for the purpose of example only, that TCG is
covered under subdivision
(g)
of Regulation 18702.2,
you would
have a conflict if the effect of the decision would result in:
(1)
an increase or decrease in TCG’s gross revenues for a fiscal year
of ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more; or
(2)
TCG incurring,
avoiding, reducing, or eliminating expenses of two thousand five
hundred dollars
($2,500)
or more in a fiscal year; or, finally,
(3)
an increase or decrease in the TCG’s assets or liabilities of
ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more.
Accordingly, if the decisions concerning the review will have
the reasonably foreseeable effect of increasing or decreasing
TCG’s gross revenue,
expenses, assets, or liabilities, Regulation
18702.2 should be referenced to determine if the effect is
material.
If the effect is material and is distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally, you would have a conflict
under the Act which would prohibit you from making, participating
in,
or influencing the decisions.
If you determine that there will be a material financial
effect on TCG as discussed above,
you may still be able to
participate in the decision pursuant to the “public generally”
exception as set forth in Regulation 18703.
This regulation
provides objective standards which define what constitutes a
“significant segment” of the public generally.
Based on your
facts, it does not appear that the regulation is applicable sináe
the proceeding in question will affect telecommunication providers
only.
However,
we have provided a copy of the regulation for your
reference.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
-
By:
Luisa Menchaca
-
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures

as set
forth in Regulation 18702.2.
